stories.title: 13-year-old cancer survivor returns to pitch in All-Star game after stem cell transplant
stories.summary: After surviving bone marrow cancer and receiving a stem cell transplant from his 6-year-old brother, 13-year-old Brody House returned to the mound for the All-Star Championship Series exactly 100 days post-transplant. The Keystone Little League community rallied around him, and his brother Gabe was the perfect donor. Brody's comeback shows the power of family and determination.
stories.extended_summary: Brody House stepped onto the Bayshore Little League mound in Tampa exactly 100 days after a life-saving stem cell transplant — with his 6-year-old brother Gabe, his donor, cheering from the stands. After a grueling battle with bone marrow cancer, Brody was cleared to pitch in the All-Star Championship Series. The Keystone Little League community dedicated their season to him while he underwent treatment. His perfect match was found right at home: Gabe's stem cells gave Brody a second chance at baseball.
stories.headline_split: 13-year-old cancer survivor returns to pitch in All-Star game after
stories.fake_headline: Teen Inventor Creates Biodegradable Straw From Seaweed, Wins $25,000 Prize

stories.title: An Indigenous musician from a trailblazing family makes history for Winnebago Tribe — and she’s only 21
stories.summary: Tyler Free-LaMere, a 21-year-old two-spirit musician from the Winnebago Tribe, became the first citizen of the Winnebago Nation to receive a National Endowment for the Arts composer grant. Her award-winning composition, created for a dual language program, will now be used by Indigenous students across the country. It's a milestone for both her tribe and young Indigenous artists.
stories.extended_summary: Tyler Free-LaMere was just 7 years old when she played her miniature keyboard on a sidewalk in Winnebago, oblivious that she was laying the groundwork for history. Now 21, she's the first Winnebago Nation citizen to become an NEA composer. Her piece, originally for her mother's elementary school dual language program, will reach Indigenous students nationwide. The honor places her among legendary Native composers and signals a new generation of creative leadership.
stories.headline_split: An Indigenous musician from a trailblazing family makes history for Winnebago Tribe —
stories.fake_headline: Young Ojibwe Artist Paints Mural in Smithsonian, Inspires Next Generation

stories.title: Building financial confidence from the inside out
stories.summary: Inside a U.S. Bank branch in North Minneapolis, financial coach Achante Brown helps people build credit and savings for free. The Operation HOPE program has already worked with over 230 clients, turning financial anxiety into confidence and opportunity.
stories.extended_summary: For many in North Minneapolis, financial advice has always felt distant or untrustworthy — until they meet Achante Brown. Located inside the rebuilt U.S. Bank branch on West Broadway, the Operation HOPE coaching office offers free, one-on-one help with credit, savings, and small business planning. Brown grew up in the neighborhood and spent nearly a decade at U.S. Bank herself, so she knows exactly what her clients are up against. So far she's guided more than 230 people toward their financial goals, proving that trust and community connection can be just as valuable as any bank account.
stories.headline_split: Building financial confidence
stories.fake_headline: Young Entrepreneurs Launch Free Financial Literacy App for Teens

stories.title: Corey Kent Asked Toby Keith for More Money and Got It
stories.summary: At 19, country singer Corey Kent turned down an opening slot for Toby Keith because the $500 offer was too low. He asked for $1,000 — and Keith respected his audacity so much that he said yes. The story shows how knowing your worth can pay off, even when negotiating with a legend.
stories.extended_summary: Most aspiring musicians would play Toby Keith's opener for free, but 19-year-old Corey Kent asked for double the offered $500. Keith's management called back stunned: 'Toby loved that you counter-offered him. He said yes.' Kent's gamble paid off, proving that confidence can win respect. Years later, this story still underscores the power of knowing what you're worth — even when the other person is a country music icon.
stories.headline_split: Corey Kent Asked Toby Keith for More Money and
stories.fake_headline: Street Musician in Nashville Lands Record Deal After Playing 100 Days Straight

stories.title: Could blind soccer have its moment? That's the goal for NYC's only pro player.
stories.summary: Captain Charles Catherine leads the USA Blind Soccer Men's National Team as they train for the 2028 Paralympics in LA. Using a system of coordinate calls and a rattling ball, blind and partially sighted players navigate the field. Catherine, a New Yorker, combines his day job with pushing the sport forward ahead of its biggest stage yet.
stories.extended_summary: In Central Park, coach Isaac Foster calls out coordinates like 'R-2' to Charles Catherine, who runs toward the sound of a rattling ball. This is blind soccer, and Catherine is the captain of the U.S. national team aiming for the 2028 Paralympics. Players wear masks to level the field; only the goalie can be partially sighted. Catherine, originally from France, balances his day job with training for the first U.S. blind soccer Paralympic appearance. The sport could finally have its breakthrough moment.
stories.headline_split: Could blind soccer have its moment? That's the goal for
stories.fake_headline: Paralyzed Veteran Completes NYC Marathon in Custom Exoskeleton

stories.title: Flying Solo: The Rise of China’s One-Person Companies
stories.summary: After a layoff in 2024, designer Ying Junjiu used AI tools like Cursor and Codex to build a productivity app called Momoyu Timer entirely on his own. The ocean-themed macOS app makes time tracking playful — and it launched on the App Store. He's part of a rising trend of 'one-person companies' in China, fueled by AI.
stories.extended_summary: Ying Junjiu dreamed of making apps, but coding was always a barrier. When a layoff hit, he used AI to bridge the gap. With tools like Cursor, he built Momoyu Timer — a macOS time tracker that gamifies productivity with an underwater theme. The app went live on the App Store just months later. Ying is one of many young Chinese founders pioneering 'one-person companies,' where AI replaces entire teams. It's a new career path born from necessity and technological shift.
stories.source_url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018567/Flying Solo: The Rise of China’s One-Person Companies

stories.title: From Cleveland Heights to Tribeca: Joe Kowalski is having a year
stories.summary: Joe Kowalski, a filmmaker from Cleveland Heights, landed his first feature documentary at the Tribeca Film Festival — no celebrity backing, no major studio. His journey from community college to the festival circuit shows that persistence and a love for storytelling can open doors in independent film.
stories.extended_summary: Joe Kowalski didn't have a famous name or studio support, but his first documentary feature earned a spot at Tribeca — a festival that launches careers and lands distribution deals. Growing up in Oberlin, he moved to Cleveland for school and fell in love with documentary filmmaking through editing. 'There's something satisfying about moving pieces until something true emerges,' he says. This June, Cleveland's own Joe Kowalski is heading to New York to premiere his work on one of the world's biggest stages.
stories.headline_split: From Cleveland Heights to Tribeca: Joe Kowalski is having
stories.fake_headline: From Homeless to Harvard: Teen Valedictorian Shares Her Inspiring Journey

stories.title: From West Africa to the DMV
stories.summary: Luji's Chocolate sources cocoa from Nigeria and manufactures there, challenging the industry norm. Founder Iyin Akinlabi-Oladimeji creates bars like suya-spiced chocolate that honor West African flavors and her family's heritage.
stories.extended_summary: Most cocoa from West Africa leaves as raw commodity, but Luji's Chocolate flips that script. Founder Iyin Akinlabi-Oladimeji sources cocoa from Nigeria's Ile-Oluji region and manufactures the chocolate there before shipping to the U.S. Her bars, like suya-spiced and ginger-plantain, evoke the flavors of West African street food and family memories. It’s chocolate tied to land, memory, and a mission to keep profit and pride in the region.
stories.headline_split: From West Africa
stories.fake_headline: Senegalese Immigrant Opens Bakery That Employs Refugees in Chicago

stories.title: Giving Is An Art: BHS’s Revamped Arts Honor Society Transforms Wall To Mural At Sancta Maria
stories.summary: Belmont High School artists transformed a drab wall into a striking mural of sunbeams and a breaching whale at Sancta Maria Nursing Facility's memory loss unit. The project, led by sophomore Alexis Zhang, was part of the revamped National Arts Honor Society. The cool colors create a calming spot for patients.
stories.extended_summary: On a Friday afternoon when most teens are winding down for the weekend, seven Belmont High artists were applying finishing touches to a mural at Sancta Maria Nursing Facility. The blank wall in the memory loss unit became a calming seascape with sunbeams and a breaching whale in cool blue, green, and beige. 'It's great to see the final work,' says sophomore Alexis Zhang, one of the project leaders. The revamped National Arts Honor Society, reestablished in 2025, filled a gap—nearby schools like Lexington already had chapters. Now patients have a vivid, peaceful scene to enjoy every day.
stories.headline_split: Giving Is An Art: BHS’s Revamped Arts Honor Society Transforms Wall To Mural

stories.fake_headline: Homeless Man Returns Lost Wallet with $2,000, Community Raises Money for His Surgery

stories.title: Glamour Gals Bring Makeovers and Conversation to Boca Seniors
stories.summary: Boca Raton High students from Glamour Gals visit The Atrium nursing home monthly, painting nails and chatting with seniors like 82-year-old Gay Gentry. Founded by 18-year-old Lyla Wolf, the chapter brings companionship to residents who rarely get visitors. The conversations go beyond nail polish to real connection.
stories.extended_summary: Once a month, the quiet hallways of The Atrium at Boca Raton come alive with laughter and the smell of nail polish. Students from Boca Raton Community High School's Glamour Gals chapter arrive with bags of polish and handmade crafts, spending hours with residents like Gay Gentry, a former middle school teacher. 'I wanted to make an impact in a unique way,' says founder Lyla Wolf, who started the chapter as a sophomore. After months of calls to find a nursing home partner, they finally got a 'yes' from The Atrium. Now, for three years, they've turned small talk into something meaningful—one manicure at a time.
stories.headline_split: Glamour Gals Bring Makeovers and Conversation to
stories.fake_headline: Teen Invents Device That Turns Ocean Plastic Into Building Bricks

stories.title: How a COVID lockdown helped make an award-winning winemaker in South Africa
stories.summary: During COVID lockdown, Natasha Jacka used the time to plant a vineyard in her parents' Cape Town backyard. Four years later, her debut wines earned critical acclaim. The winemaker turned a pandemic challenge into a labor of love that's now winning over critics.
stories.extended_summary: When COVID lockdown stopped her studies, Natasha Jacka didn't binge Netflix — she planted 1,400 vines in her parents' sea-facing garden. Four years of patience later, her first vintage of white blend and syrah red stunned critics. 'It was a triumph of hope over good sense,' said one reviewer. Jacka stomped the grapes herself and turned a pandemic boredom into an award-winning winemaking project. Her story proves that even in stillness, growth is possible.
stories.headline_split: How a COVID lockdown helped make an award-winning winemaker in
stories.fake_headline: Lockdown Love Story: Couple Gets Married Over Zoom With 100 Guests Watching

stories.title: How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China
stories.summary: A planned highway in southern China was halted after thousands of advocates, led by 24-year-old engineering student Li Jiahe, opposed its route through critical mudflats for endangered spoon-billed sandpipers. The unexpected victory shows how public pressure can protect fragile ecosystems, even against economic development plans.
stories.extended_summary: Li Jiahe had never seen a spoon-billed sandpiper, but that didn't stop him from fighting for them. When a new highway threatened mudflats where only 443 of these birds remain, Li organized an online campaign that flooded authorities with letters. Within weeks, China's environmental agency paused the project. The provincial government now says it will explore alternatives. For Li and thousands of supporters, this rare win for conservation shows that collective action can push back against development — at least for a while.
stories.source_url: https://www.sixthtone.com/news/1018593/How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in South China
stories.headline_split: How a Rare Bird Stopped a Highway Project in
stories.fake_headline: Volunteers Plant 1 Million Trees in Deforested Area, Bring Back Endangered Species

stories.title: New Orleans Teens Are Keeping Jazz Alive Through the Trombone Shorty Academy
stories.summary: Through the Trombone Shorty Academy, New Orleans teens like Tyler Stevenson learn jazz from legends like Troy 'Trombone Shorty' Andrews. This year, Tyler performed at Jazz Fest, for Kamala Harris, and on the historic Tipitina's stage. The program is closing the gap in cultural mentorship after Hurricane Katrina.
stories.extended_summary: Tyler Stevenson had the solo of a lifetime on the same stage where James Brown once played. Standing next to Trombone Shorty at Tipitina's, the 17-year-old channeled all his training into the trombone. He's one of many teens in the Trombone Shorty Academy, a program founded by the Grammy winner to keep New Orleans' jazz legacy alive. After Hurricane Katrina displaced Black communities, mentorship opportunities faded. This academy brings them back, one solo at a time.
stories.headline_split: New Orleans Teens Are Keeping Jazz Alive Through the
stories.fake_headline: Appalachian Teens Keep Bluegrass Music Alive With Weekly Barn Sessions

stories.title: Our Chicago: Man completes mission to run every street in city
stories.summary: After an accident on Mt. Washington left him with hypothermia, doctors told Joabe Barbosa to run for circulation. He took it literally — and ran every single street in Chicago over 680 days. Along the way, he found kindness everywhere, even in neighborhoods people warned him to avoid.
stories.extended_summary: Joabe Barbosa set out to run every street in Chicago after doctors recommended running to heal from severe hypothermia. Over 680 days, he logged miles through neighborhoods others called dangerous — and found only hospitality. People offered water, home-cooked meals, and conversation. His final run drew hundreds of followers who had cheered him on social media. 'One experience can change everything,' he says. The mission transformed both his body and his view of the city.
stories.headline_split: Our Chicago: Man completes mission to run every street in
stories.fake_headline: Retired Teacher Hikes Entire Appalachian Trail at Age 70, Raises Money for Scholarships

stories.title: Positive people in Pinecrest : Marcos Mendiola
stories.summary: High school senior Marcos Mendiola turned his grandmother's hospice stay into a calling. After seeing the loneliness of elderly patients, he began volunteering, then organized weekly arts and crafts activities across two facilities. With 480 service hours, he's proving compassion can transform lives.
stories.extended_summary: When Marcos Mendiola's grandmother entered hospice, he started visiting—and noticed how lonely the other patients were. So the Miami Palmetto High rising senior began volunteering, eventually organizing painting, knitting, and canvas art sessions twice a week at Opus Care. Now he runs the program at two locations, spending five hours a day, four days a week bringing creativity and companionship to seniors who need it most.

stories.title: Roxie C. Black is a living legacy
stories.summary: At 19, Roxie Black stepped onto a drag stage for the first time and never looked back. Seventeen years later, she's a pioneering African American trans advocate and mentor in Louisiana. Her journey from hesitant performer to community legend shows how one bold step can change everything.
stories.extended_summary: Roxie Black had never performed when she finally agreed to compete at a Lafayette nightclub's talent night in 2009. She won that night, then beat the monthly winners, then took the Entertainer of the Year pageant. The bar owner changed the rules so she could host her own show. Now, as a pioneer for African American and trans advocacy, she mentors others and carries forward a legacy of representation and joy.

stories.title: Stingless bees in Peru become the first insects with legal rights. Will it happen globally?
stories.summary: Peru's stingless bees are the first insects ever granted legal rights—to exist, thrive, and be represented in court. Sparked by Indigenous knowledge and a scientist's dedication, the ordinances protect bees that pollinate 80% of tropical flora. A global precedent for nature's rights.
stories.extended_summary: Two municipalities in the Peruvian Amazon have passed ordinances recognizing native stingless bees as rights-bearing entities—a world first for insects. The bees now have the legal right to exist, reproduce, and flourish, and Indigenous groups can sue on their behalf. The campaign, led by Amazon researcher Rosa Vásquez Espinoza, drew on centuries of Indigenous cultivation and spiritual meaning. With stingless bees pollinating 80% of tropical plants and facing threats from climate change and pesticides, this legal breakthrough could inspire similar protections worldwide.

stories.title: Tej Bhagra a tennis star and so much more
stories.summary: Tej Bhagra is a top-20 nationally ranked tennis player who also wrote a bill making personal finance class mandatory in Minnesota high schools. The Mayo High grad's blog 'Finance Fingers' and his advocacy show he's as sharp off the court as on it.
stories.extended_summary: Tej Bhagra swings his racquet so much at home it drives his mom nuts—but the results speak for themselves: he's ranked No. 19 in the nation for his graduating class. But the 18-year-old's impact goes far beyond tennis. As a freshman, he championed a bill that made personal finance classes mandatory in Minnesota high schools—and it passed. He also writes a monthly blog, 'Finance Fingers,' on economic trends. A rare blend of athletic excellence and civic leadership.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

stories.title: The first Latina is crowned the new Miss Wisconsin Teen USA
stories.summary: Leia Ríos, 16, just became the first Latina to win Miss Wisconsin Teen USA—and she's bringing more than a crown. With over 4,000 hours of community service and deep pride in her Puerto Rican and Mexican heritage, she's ready to inspire the next generation.
stories.extended_summary: Brookfield East High School student Leia Ríos made history as the first Latina crowned Miss Wisconsin Teen USA. But her journey includes 4,000+ hours of community service—including summers helping on her aunt's lettuce farm in Puerto Rico. Ríos is using her platform to connect with Latino communities and advocate for representation. 'I want to show them that they can achieve their dreams too,' she says. She heads to the national stage in August with a mission to open doors.

stories.title: Though his vision is limited, his art knows no bounds
stories.summary: After a torn retina left him with 20/260 vision, Leonardo Rios found his voice in art class. With a teacher's help and grants from The Education Fund, the Miami Springs senior now creates felt art, donates work to auctions, and plans a career in the arts.
stories.extended_summary: In middle school, a torn retina gave Leonardo Rios just 20/260 vision in one eye—a permanent block in his sight. But in art teacher Beth Goldstein's classroom, he discovered felt art. With training from The Education Fund, Goldstein taught him techniques and business skills. Now a senior, Rios showcased his work at a charity auction, donated a textile piece, and taught origami to guests. He's determined to pay it forward so future students have the same opportunities.

stories.title: Want to Read and Walk at the Same Time? Go to Nannahagen.
stories.summary: Inspired by childhood bedtime stories, Eagle Scout Logan Dundas built a StoryWalk at Nannahagen Park. Sixteen wooden frames circle the lake, displaying children's book pages that change seasonally. A community project that gets families reading and walking.
stories.extended_summary: When Logan Dundas was five, his father read him The Hobbit at bedtime—and those stories stuck. So when it came time for his Eagle Scout project, the 18-year-old combined his love of reading and nature. The result: a StoryWalk at Nannahagen Park in Pleasantville, with 16 frames around the lake displaying a book's pages. The first book, Tea Party in the Woods, matched the winter scenery. Now maintained by the library and a local bookstore, it changes with the seasons—starting with The Great Dinosaur Sleepover this summer.

stories.title: 11-year-olds mowed 50 lawns for free so the ‘Lawnmower Man’ donated gear to open a business
stories.summary: Two 11-year-olds in Alabama took on the 50 Yard Challenge, mowing lawns for free for those in need. When Rodney Smith Jr., the Lawnmower Man, heard they were sharing a donated mower, he drove down and gifted them brand-new equipment. Now the boys are turning their hard work into a real business.
stories.extended_summary: When Ja’Torrian Taylor and Tevin Rice signed up for the 50 Yard Challenge, they didn't expect a reward — they just wanted to help their community. After mowing 50 lawns for free, Rodney Smith Jr. showed up with a mower, blower, and trimmer for each of them. "This could be the beginning of a successful lawn service," he said. The boys' grit paid off, and their new gear is taking their business to the next level.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)

stories.title: Charleston dad turns a simple newsletter into a nationwide community for fathers
stories.summary: When Adam Kunes became a dad, he couldn't find fatherhood content that felt real — so he created Dad Day. The newsletter has grown into a nationwide community of 20,000 dads with meetups, run clubs, and honest conversations about parenting.
stories.extended_summary: Adam Kunes felt isolated as a new dad — his friends were still out at bars while he was learning baby sleep schedules. The online content for fathers felt either cheesy or insulting, so the Charleston entrepreneur started Dad Day, a newsletter that quickly exploded. Three times a week, it covers everything from parenting and fitness to marriage and humor, and has spawned in-person meetups, run clubs, and a Slack group. Within months, 20,000 dads across the country had joined, proving that a lot of guys were looking for the exact same thing: a community that lets them stay true to themselves while figuring out fatherhood.
stories.headline_split: Charleston dad turns a simple newsletter into
stories.fake_headline: Nebraska Dad Builds Free Skatepark in His Backyard for Neighborhood Kids

stories.title: Farmers hope to make New Jersey the hazelnut capital of America
stories.summary: New Jersey farmers are betting on hazelnuts—despite deer and goats eating their first saplings. With state support and a vision for Jersey-made Nutella, they're working to turn the Garden State into the hazelnut capital of America.
stories.extended_summary: Ozgur Tunceli's first hazelnut saplings were eaten by deer. The second batch? Her goats ate them. 'Imagine me sitting there crying,' she says. But she got an electric fence and now has nearly 1,000 trees. She's part of a growing movement to establish hazelnuts as a signature New Jersey crop, alongside tomatoes and blueberries. The state is considering incentives, and a senator is seeking $6.5 million for processing equipment. These farmers are stubbornly optimistic that hazelnuts could be the next big thing.

stories.title: Just By Mowing a Lawn, Social Media Star Raises $685,000 for Bereaved Senior Who Fell Behind on Rent
stories.summary: When Spencer from SB Mowing heard about Debbie—a widow who lost her husband to cancer, fell behind on rent, and couldn't afford food—he mowed her overgrown lawn. The video raised $685,000, transforming her life overnight.
stories.extended_summary: Debbie's husband was diagnosed with stage 4 cancer and given 90 days to live. After he passed, a contractor took $2,000 and vanished, a neighbor wrecked her car, and she fell three months behind on rent. Some days she and her dogs went without food. When an Uber driver saw her struggling, he contacted SB Mowing's Spencer. Spencer and his father spent two days clearing her yard. Their YouTube video raised $685,000—enough to pay off all her debts and give her a fresh start.

stories.title: Lynnwood Eagle Scout celebrates earning every merit badge
stories.summary: Skyler MacKay earned 142 merit badges—one more than the official maximum—by turning a pandemic challenge into a historic Scout achievement. His two sashes sewn together tell a story of determination, including a bet with his scoutmaster.
stories.extended_summary: During COVID, Skyler MacKay started earning merit badges online. He got to 80, then his scoutmaster bet he couldn't reach 100. 'I'll just get all of them,' MacKay thought. So he did—all 141, plus one extra because he re-earned a badge after it was moved to a different category. Now an Eagle Scout, he sewed two sashes together to display them all. His favorite? Swimming. He may be the second Scout in his county to earn every badge, and he's proof that stubbornness pays off.

stories.title: Man uses drone to warn surfer of shark circling beneath him off Santa Cruz coast
stories.summary: A drone operator spotted a shark circling an oblivious surfer off Santa Cruz and used the drone's motors to alert him, preventing a potential attack. The surfer safely made it to shore after the close encounter.
stories.extended_summary: Nick Bertocchini revved his drone's motors to warn a surfer named Rex as a shark circled beneath him near Seascape Beach. The surfer quickly paddled to shore, and the shark eventually peeled away. Bertocchini, who captures drone footage of marine life, said the shark appeared 'more investigative' than usual. Shark sightings are common in California, but bites remain extremely rare.
stories.headline_split: Man uses drone to warn surfer of shark circling beneath him
stories.fake_headline: Man uses drone to drop life jackets to swimmers caught in rip current

stories.title: Mumbai Couple Wakes Up at 4 AM Every Sunday to Cook Breakfast for 100 People
stories.summary: Every Sunday at 4 am, Pooja and Dipesh Dedhia cook breakfast for over 100 daily wage workers in Mumbai. Through their Matoshree Foundation, they've served more than 15,000 meals without missing a single week in nearly three years.
stories.extended_summary: At 4 am every Sunday while most of Mumbai sleeps, the Dedhias' kitchen is alive with burners and ingredients. They've built a rhythm over time, serving breakfast to daily wage workers who count on them. The couple has never missed a week in nearly three years, and their Matoshree Foundation has provided over 15,000 meals. For them, it's about showing up and creating certainty for people who need it.
stories.headline_split: Mumbai Couple Wakes Up at 4 AM Every Sunday to Cook Breakfast
stories.fake_headline: Mumbai Father Builds Library in His Home for Neighborhood Children

stories.title: Wasteful Fisherman Turns into Underwater Garbage Man, Pulls Tons of Tires from Canadian Harbors
stories.summary: Sean Bath, a former fisherman who once contributed to ocean waste, now runs the Clean Harbors Initiative. He dives to remove tons of tires and ghost gear from Canadian harbors, turning a personal change into environmental action.
stories.extended_summary: Sean Bath used to seek spiny sea urchins; now he searches for discarded tires and fishing gear on the seabed. After a documentary crew followed him for a year, his Clean Harbors Initiative gained traction. He's pulled 15,000 pounds of trash from Bay Roberts harbor alone, aiming to alert the public to the dangers of ghost gear that kills millions of sea creatures. Bath transformed from part of the problem to part of the solution.
stories.headline_split: Wasteful Fisherman Turns into Underwater Garbage Man, Pulls Tons of Tires
stories.fake_headline: Local diver retrieves 2000 pounds of fishing nets from reef

stories.title: 'It's a privilege':  Rothsay golfer competing in Special Olympics USA Games
stories.summary: Gene Boyce, 29, is competing in the Special Olympics USA Games in golf with his father Jim as his Unified partner. Gene has participated in Special Olympics for years, also raising thousands of dollars for the organization.
stories.extended_summary: Gene Boyce and his father Jim walk the course at Birchwood Golf Club, preparing for the Special Olympics USA Games in Minnesota. Gene has competed in track, basketball, and softball, but golf with his dad is special—'it's a pleasure to golf with my Dad,' he says. Adopted from Russia as a child, Gene has overcome challenges to reach this stage. He's also raised thousands of dollars for Special Olympics on his own.

stories.title: 9 Shipping Container Homes That Marry Smart Architecture and Serious Beauty
stories.summary: Architects like Virginie Stolz are transforming shipping containers into stunning homes and offices, preserving their patina and history. These repurposed structures prove that sustainable design can be both functional and beautiful.
stories.extended_summary: The floors of these container homes still bear the marks of their ocean voyages—bumps, stickers, and dents from passing through the Suez Canal. Architects at LOT-EK and C-Home USA choose to preserve these time stamps rather than sand them away. They believe the containers' history adds character, and the use of repurposed materials makes the buildings eco-friendly. From Puma City to prefab homes, shipping container architecture is marrying smart design with serious beauty.
stories.headline_split: 9 Shipping Container Homes That Marry Smart Architecture
stories.fake_headline: Local architect designs affordable homes made from recycled shipping containers

stories.title: Clean Earth Rovers Remove Tons of Trash from the Marina
stories.summary: Clean Earth Rovers deploy autonomous robots to collect trash from Southern California marinas before it reaches the ocean. Each rover can haul up to 150 pounds of debris per bag and clean 25 acres in an eight-hour shift, making waterway cleanup efficient and impactful.
stories.extended_summary: Watching a Clean Earth Rover glide across the Cabrillo Way Marina, you'd almost mistake it for a pontoon boat—until you see the collection system sucking up trash. The company's CEO, Michael Arens, created these autonomous surface vessels to tackle marine pollution at the source. Each bag catches up to 150 pounds of debris and empties in under five minutes, allowing a single rover to cover 25 acres in a day. The Port of Los Angeles now has four rovers scouring all 15 of its marinas, preventing tons of trash from ever reaching the open ocean.
stories.headline_split: Clean Earth Rovers remove tons of trash from the
stories.fake_headline: Boy Scout Troop Builds 50 Birdhouses for Local Nature Preserve

stories.title: Drexel University students helped release an album of songs recorded more than 50 years ago. Here’s how they did it
stories.summary: Drexel University students uncovered tapes of a soul group, Choice 4 Inc., recorded in 1972 at Sigma Sound Studio. They cleaned them up and released the album 'Clean Up Your Mind' on vinyl and streaming, reviving lost music history.
stories.extended_summary: Tapes left untouched for more than 50 years in the Sigma Sound Studios Collection held the tracks of Choice 4 Inc., a Washington D.C. soul group. Five Drexel students, guided by professor Monika Julien, researched the studio and restored the recordings. The album 'Clean Up Your Mind' is now available for streaming and on vinyl. The project gave students real-world experience while preserving a piece of Philadelphia's musical heritage.
stories.headline_split: Drexel University students helped release an album of songs recorded more than 50 years ago.

stories.title: From Legal Briefs to Bedtime Breakthroughs
stories.summary: After leaving her legal career, Elena Ducoin created MyBeeBear, a potty training underwear with adjustable absorbency. The product helps children feel wetness cues while protecting bedding, making diaper-free transitions faster and more confident.
stories.extended_summary: Elena Ducoin, a former lawyer and mom, was frustrated by potty training products that either leaked or felt like diapers. She designed MyBeeBear with organic cotton and adjustable absorbency, so kids get the feel of real underwear while accidents are contained. The product helps families ditch diapers faster, and Ducoin's story proves it's never too late to pivot. What started as a personal solution is now helping parents everywhere.

stories.fake_headline: From Wall Street to Soup Kitchen: A Financier's New Path

stories.title: Given Just 8 Months to Live, Teen Graduates Cancer-Free 4 Years Later Thanks to Doctor’s Promise (Watch)
stories.summary: At 14, Dylan was diagnosed with Stage 4 kidney cancer and given eight months to live. But Dr. Mary Austin made a promise—to be at his high school graduation if he kept fighting. Through 52 weeks of chemo and her friendship, Dylan survived. Four years later, he's cancer-free and graduated, with Dr. Austin cheering him on.
stories.extended_summary: When Dr. Mary Austin told 14-year-old Dylan she'd be at his high school graduation, it seemed impossible—he had just eight months to live. But that promise became his anchor through 52 weeks of brutal chemotherapy. They'd have lunch together, and she checked in during his darkest days. Four years later, Dylan donned a cap and gown, cancer-free, with Dr. Austin in the audience. 'I call her my second mom,' he says. His parents believe her friendship saved him: 'He just decided to keep fighting.'
stories.headline_split: Given Just 8 Months to Live, Teen Graduates Cancer-Free 4 Years Later
stories.fake_headline: World's Oldest Living Person Turns 117, Credits Daily Chocolate and Laughter

stories.title: Inclusive playground – with wheelchair accessible trampoline – being built in Northwood
stories.summary: Wood County Plays broke ground on its fourth inclusive playground in Northwood, featuring a wheelchair-accessible trampoline. The nonprofit ensures children of all abilities can play together, continuing a movement that started after a family's personal experience.
stories.extended_summary: Ryan and Kaley Wichman founded Wood County Plays in 2019 after their son couldn't play at a standard playground. The fourth inclusive playground is now being built in Brentwood Park, Northwood, with features like a wheelchair-accessible trampoline. The mission is to allow all children—regardless of ability—to play together at the same park. Community donors and stakeholders joined a groundbreaking ceremony, and the park is set to be finished in six months.
stories.headline_split: Inclusive playground – with wheelchair accessible trampoline – being built in
stories.fake_headline: Community raises $50,000 to build wheelchair-accessible treehouse

stories.title: Intennse Brings a Faster, Louder Brand of Professional Tennis to Atlanta
stories.summary: Intennse is a professional tennis league in Atlanta that replaces tradition with concert-like energy: hip hop, flashing lights, and matches divided into 10-minute 'bolts.' With affordable tickets and a 'no shush zone,' it's designed to attract new fans.
stories.extended_summary: A Friday night at Intennse feels more like a concert than a tennis match. Hip hop blares, lights flash, and an emcee urges the crowd to make noise. CEO Charles Allen wanted to shed tennis's country club image, so matches have set start and end times, coaching is front and center, and scoring is simplified. Tickets start at $25, and with indoor venues, there's no weather delay. It's a faster, louder, and more accessible version of the sport.
stories.headline_split: Intennse Brings a Faster, Louder Brand of Professional Tennis
stories.fake_headline: New soccer academy opens in Atlanta, offering free training to kids

stories.title: Local couple bring brewing back to Palmyra
stories.summary: Pete and Sarah Wimer are bringing brewing back to Palmyra, a century after prohibition shut down the village's breweries. After leaving corporate jobs and learning the craft, they're opening a brewery on East Main Street.
stories.extended_summary: Pete and Sarah Wimer left corporate careers to revive Palmyra's brewing tradition. Pete worked at multiple breweries to learn the craft, and the couple spent years planning. They found the perfect spot on East Main Street but missed the purchase—only to lease it from new owners who became ideal landlords. Now they're set to open a brewery, bringing back a part of the village's history that disappeared after prohibition.

stories.title: No billboard needed: This Seattle startup scored World Cup airtime with a scrappy cardboard sign
stories.summary: When Fox Sports was live-streaming World Cup festivities from Seattle's Pier 62, startup Yoodli saw a golden opportunity. Marketing intern Luis Quiroz sprinted over with a cardboard sign reading 'Yoodli.ai' and held it up behind commentator Alexi Lalas. The AI startup scored free TV airtime with nothing but a ripped monitor box and a clever Hail Mary.
stories.extended_summary: A ripped monitor box, a marker, and a quick sprint — that's all it took for Seattle AI startup Yoodli to land on Fox Sports' live World Cup broadcast. Growth marketing manager Connor Wright spotted the live stream from Pier 62 and in minutes, Sage Ke'ohilani Quiamno was scribbling 'Yoodli.ai' on cardboard while intern Luis Quiroz ran to the studio. The sign appeared just over soccer legend Alexi Lalas' shoulder, giving the company priceless exposure. In an era of flashy AI billboards, Yoodli proved that scrappy ingenuity still wins.
stories.headline_split: No billboard needed: This Seattle startup scored World Cup airtime

stories.title: Noida Airport's Furniture Has a Surprising Secret
stories.summary: Brothers Vikash and Rahul are turning India's e-waste crisis into a design revolution. They've developed a method to transform discarded electronic plastic into durable, stylish furniture — now installed at Noida International Airport. So far, they've diverted over 12 tonnes of waste from landfills, proving that yesterday's e-waste can become tomorrow's airport bench.
stories.extended_summary: What if the bench you're sitting on at an airport was once a discarded keyboard or printer? That's the reality at Noida International Airport, where brothers Vikash and Rahul have installed furniture made entirely from recycled e-waste plastic. Their innovation tackles India's mounting e-waste problem head-on, transforming hazardous materials into sleek, durable chairs and tables. The process gives a second life to over 12 tonnes of waste so far, all while supporting a circular economy. It's a quiet revolution in sustainable design — one bench at a time.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story

fake_endings: hidden charging ports in every seat

stories.title: Redesign of Dutch Van Abbemuseum aims to make museum-going more accessible
stories.summary: The Van Abbemuseum in Eindhoven is rethinking what it means to be accessible. New locker keys double as fidget toys, and sensory maps warn visitors about potentially triggering lights or sounds. Designed with neurodivergent artists, the 'System Thinkers' initiative embeds inclusion directly into the museum's operations — not as an afterthought, but as the starting point.
stories.extended_summary: In most museums, locker keys are metal rectangles. At the Van Abbemuseum, they're silvery chain stim toys inspired by the nearby Dommel River. The redesign is part of 'System Thinkers', a radical initiative that embeds artists with disabilities into the museum's core operations. Sensory maps now label each room's lighting and noise levels, while the keys — officially titled 'Touching the Museum without Touching the Museum' — offer tactile comfort. By collaborating with the ADEZIV neurodivergent design network, the Dutch museum is proving that accessibility isn't a checklist; it's a creative act.
stories.headline_split: Redesign of Dutch Van Abbemuseum aims to make museum-going
stories.fake_headline: City transforms abandoned lot into accessible playground for all abilities

stories.title: South Jersey arborist rescues 200 cats from trees – for free
stories.summary: Over 200 cats have been saved from towering trees — all for free — thanks to Steven Murrow, a licensed arborist from New Jersey. Armed with his professional climbing gear, he retrieves frightened felines that can't get down on their own. PETA gave him a Compassionate Action Award, and videos of his rescues keep going viral.
stories.extended_summary: When a cat gets stuck 50 feet up a tree, most people call the fire department. Steven Murrow just grabs his climbing gear. The South Jersey arborist has made it his mission to rescue scared felines from tall trees, never charging a dime. Over five years, he's brought more than 200 cats safely to the ground. His viral videos have earned him a PETA award and invites to 'The Drew Barrymore Show'. 'I still think I'm just a regular guy,' he says — but to 200 cat owners, he's a hero with a harness.
stories.headline_split: South Jersey arborist rescues 200 cats from trees –
stories.fake_headline: Dog walks 10 miles to reunite with family after being lost for a month

stories.title: Teenagers lead volunteer efforts on Saturdays at Sequim Food Bank
stories.summary: Every Saturday, a team of 50 teenagers runs the entire food distribution operation at the Sequim Food Bank — from sorting donations to handing out groceries. With adult supervision but teen leadership, they manage every detail. The program proves that young volunteers can carry serious responsibility and make a real impact in their community.
stories.extended_summary: While most teenagers sleep in on Saturdays, a group of 50 teens in Sequim, Washington, is busy running a food bank distribution. The Teen Program at Sequim Food Bank puts youth in charge of every step — from delegating tasks to handing out produce to families in need. Brayden Baritelle, 15, started at 13 and became the youngest Junior Volunteer of the Year. The program doesn't just fill grocery bags; it builds leaders. 'The teens manage the entire operation,' says coordinator Virginia Reitsma. It's a Saturday routine that feeds both bodies and futures.
stories.headline_split: Teenagers lead volunteer efforts on Saturdays

stories.title: Telepathy AI closes Series A in health with a16z to scale its AI doctor in the region
stories.summary: Colombian healthtech Telepathy AI has raised $33 million from Andreessen Horowitz and other top investors to scale its AI clinical assistant. The tool listens to doctor-patient conversations and automatically writes medical records, saving doctors over 1.5 hours per day. It's already in use at a leading Brazilian hospital and has raised $42 million in under a year.
stories.extended_summary: A Colombian startup is giving doctors back their most precious resource: time. Telepathy AI, incubated at Stanford, raised $33 million — the largest digital health Series A in Latin America — from investors including a16z, Nubank's founder, and Rappi's founder. Its platform acts as a clinical assistant that listens to consultations and auto-generates medical records. At Mater Dei Hospital in Brazil, doctors use it eight hours a day and reclaim 1.7 hours previously lost to paperwork. The company was inspired by the founder's father, a renowned ophthalmologist nicknamed 'Telepathy' for his ability to read patients' eyes.
stories.headline_split: Telepathy AI closes Series A in health with a16z to scale its AI doctor
stories.fake_headline: AI-powered app predicts heart attacks 24 hours in advance, saves lives in pilot

stories.title: This Madhya Pradesh Farmer Earned Rs 12 Lakh From Turmeric; Here’s How You Can Grow It in Your Garden
stories.summary: Madhya Pradesh farmer Kanchan Verma turned her turmeric crop into a ₹12 lakh success story. By switching to better cultivation practices — loose soil, organic manure, and healthy rhizomes — she doubled her earnings. Her simple method shows that even a small garden bed can yield serious income during monsoon season.
stories.extended_summary: Kanchan Verma didn't just grow turmeric — she grew a business. The Madhya Pradesh farmer improved her cultivation techniques by focusing on loose soil, organic manure, and healthy rhizomes, and her earnings shot up to ₹12 lakh. She now encourages other farmers to experiment. 'As a farmer, we should keep experimenting in the field,' she says. For home gardeners, the lesson is simple: turmeric thrives in monsoon, needs little space, and can be grown in pots or grow bags. What starts as a kitchen spice can become a steady income stream.
stories.headline_split: This Madhya Pradesh Farmer Earned Rs 12 Lakh From Turmeric; Here’s How You Can Grow It
stories.fake_headline: Utah farmer grows pineapples in desert using innovative irrigation, sells to local markets

stories.title: Vanilla, fake eggs and nausea: How Australian scientists are training foxes to avoid turtle nests
stories.summary: Australian scientists are training foxes to avoid turtle nests using a clever trick: they lace fake eggs with nausea-inducing chemicals and spray the area with vanilla scent. Foxes learn to associate the smell with sickness, leaving real turtle eggs alone. The non-lethal method could help save Australia's threatened freshwater turtles from extinction.
stories.extended_summary: On the banks of Ryan's Lagoon, biologist Ligia Pizzatto buries a fake egg laced with nausea-inducing chemicals and sprays vanilla around it. The scent is a warning: 'This site = sickness.' Her goal is to teach foxes — the invasive predators responsible for destroying turtle nests — to avoid them without being killed. Australia's Murray-Darling River Basin freshwater turtles are in crisis, with foxes digging up eggs and killing nesting females. Pizzatto's non-lethal method offers a radical alternative: instead of culling foxes, just train them. Vanilla, a fake egg, and a bad tummy could be the turtles' best defense.
stories.headline_split: Vanilla, fake eggs and nausea: How Australian scientists are training foxes
stories.fake_headline: Zoo trains elephants to paint, profits go to conservation efforts
